sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 50-year-old male, presents with chronic back pain that has been ongoing for 2 years, initially starting after moving furniture one weekend.
He describes the pain as sharp stabbing in his back, radiating down to his right leg and as far as his big toe.
He also experiences numbness and tingling in his right leg, especially to his foot.
He denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area, weakness, or loss of control of bladder or bowels.
He has tried physical therapy and strong pain medications in the past, but with limited success.
He has gained weight due to decreased activity level.
Past medical history: The patient denies any family members with spine conditions.
He quit smoking 20 years ago.
Review of systems: None mentioned.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The musculoskeletal back exam reveals no overlying erythema or ecchymosis.
There is midline tenderness at L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
The supine straight leg test is positive.
The neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
The patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
Test Results: The MRI shows disk herniation with nerve impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy.
Problem list: Herniated disk at L4-L5; radiculopathy; chronic lumbar back pain; weight gain.
Plan:
I prescribed a spinal injection with a corticosteroid medication to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
The injection will be administered under fluoroscopy to target the exact area of pain.
I will monitor the patient's response to the injection and schedule follow-up appointments as needed.
Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for a follow-up appointment to assess response to the injection and determine if additional treatments are needed.
